result:
The first thing to know is that the light in the refrigerator is only looking for an excuse to die.  It has been waiting fifteen years for someone—anyone—to finally notice the way it flickers like a moth caught in a jar.  Every time you open the door you think you’re checking the milk, but really you’re giving the bulb another tiny funeral.  You close the door too quickly to hear its small sigh of relief.  Inside the butter compartment there is a single stamp from a country that no longer exists.  You stuck it there in 2008 because you liked the tiny blue airplane, and now the glue has become a continent of brittle crystals.  If you scratched them away you would find the airplane again, still taxiing toward a runway that melted before you were born.

I keep thinking I will run out of small things to love, but the world keeps making more.  Yesterday it was the squeak the toaster gives exactly three seconds after the bread pops up, the sound of a mouse caught telling the truth.  Tomorrow it might be the way your neighbor’s wind chime insists on C-sharp even during a thunderstorm, a stubborn music teacher refusing to cancel class.  I collect them the way children collect marbles, cupped in the skirt of my attention, clacking against one another when I walk.  When I die someone will shake me and hear the chorus of my pockets sayingRemember, remember, remember.

There is a kind of silence that only arrives at 3:07 a.m., a silence with elbows.  It presses itself into the hollow behind your sternum and starts listing everything you never apologized for: the third-grade erection you couldn’t hide, the voicemail you deleted instead of returning, the plant you watered with Diet Coke to see what religion it would choose.  The silence has a filing cabinet the size of the moon.  It keeps every receipt.  If you try to argue it produces the exact temperature of the water in the motel pool the night you told your father you didn’t need his help anymore, 74.2 degrees Fahrenheit, chlorine tasting like the word almost.  You swallowed half a lap lane trying to take it back, but the damage had already put on its coat and left.

When I was eleven I believed the universe was a library and every star was a footnote.  I checked out a book titled How to Disappear Completely and Never Be Found, but I was too young to grasp the fines.  The librarian—Mrs. Alvarez with the strawberry birthmark shaped like Lake Superior—stamped the due date so hard the numbers bit through three pages: MAY 12 1999.  That night I tried to follow the instructions.  Step one: stand at the edge of the playground after dusk.  Step two: close your eyes and count backward from the total number of teeth you will ever lose.  I got to seven before my tongue found the empty socket where the molar had been, money under the pillow already spent on candy that tasted like the future.  Step three was missing, torn away by someone faster or more frightened.  I opened my eyes and the sky had already turned the page; the moon was a paper cut bleeding quiet silver.

I want to tell you about the man who delivers my groceries, how he carries the bananas as if they were still attached to the tree, how he calls me “querida” even though we have never touched.  Last week he handed me a dented can of black beans and said, “These survived a fall, they will survive you.”  I laughed because I thought it was a joke, but now the can sits on the windowsill like a small purple planet.  Every sunset it grows darker, more certain.  I have started talking to it about the weather, about the way grief is just vertigo with better scenery.  The beans listen better than my therapist, who charges a hundred and twenty dollars an hour to forget my name.  When the can finally opens I expect it will sound like a satellite door sighing after centuries of orbit: a small crush of air rushing to greet what it has always carried.

Sometimes I imagine all the phones I’ve ever owned are ringing at once in a room I can’t find.  Each screen lights up with a different year.  The 2005 Nokia plays “Take On Me” in midi, the 2013 iPhone shows a bubble-strewn screenshot of you saying goodnight for the last time, the 2021 burner buzzes with a two-factor code to an account I don’t remember creating.  The chorus is deafening.  I run through hallways that smell like burnt popcorn and library dust, searching for the door, but every knob I touch recoils into a comment section.  Someone is always wrong on the internet and the room is always twelve feet farther away.  The only thing I know for certain is that when I finally answer, the voice on the other end will be mine at age six asking if dinosaurs could feel lonely.  I will have exactly three seconds to reply before the signal drops back into the zoo of static where all our childhood questions wait for new cages.

I keep a list of ways to survive the next disaster taped inside the medicine cabinet.  Item 4: Learn to read the Braille of smoke before the alarm does.  Item 9: Keep a pair of shoes filled with water so you can carry the river with you when you run.  Item 17: Memorize one constellation that no culture has ever named; call it “The Uninsured.”  Item 23: Practice dying for sixty seconds every morning, then forget how, then forgive yourself for forgetting.  The ink has started to bleed because toothpaste is a mild acid.  Now the paper looks like it has been crying turquoise tears, which is its own kind of advice.

Tonight the moon is a fingernail clipping from the hand that first drew darkness.  I stand on the balcony wearing the sweater you left in the breakup, sleeves knitted from the promise that nothing would hurt forever.  The wind smells of train metal and lilacs, a combination that only happens in cities too tired to insist on their own stories.  Downstairs someone is learning saxophone, the same four notes circling like moths that refuse to land on the word sorry.  I count them—one, two, three, four—until they become the heartbeat of a new small thing to love: the gap between attempts, the hush where forgiveness practices its scales.  I close my eyes.  The light in the refrigerator finally goes out, soundlessly, a monk achieving enlightenment behind the mayonnaise.  Somewhere a stamp takes off from a dissolved country and lands on the envelope of my chest.  I lick it shut, address it to the library I still owe a childhood, and drop it into the mailbox of the dark.  It makes the softest thunk, like a marble landing in a skirt pocket, like a planet fitting back into its God.
